text: The optical trocar is visible, along with two 10 mm trocars and one 5 mm walking port used for the laparoscopic appendectomy procedure.
The inflamed appendix is visible. The surgeon uses a ligature in the right hand to perform serial division of the mesoappendix, effectively devascularizing the appendix.
The surgeon identifies the appendicular artery and ensures its ligation or cauterization to prevent bleeding during the appendectomy.
The surgeon performs division of the mesoappendix, cutting close to the appendix to ensure precise separation of the tissue.
The surgeon performs further dissection at the appendicular base, focusing on areas of maximal inflammation identified in the body and base region.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the lateral attachments of the appendix, revealing the thickened and inflamed peritoneum due to the inflammatory process. The appendicular base is clearly visible, showing its continuation into the cecum.
The surgeon positions endo loops at the base of the appendix, preparing for ligation and removal.
The surgeon applies commercially available endo loops, tying two loops on the patient's side of the appendix and one loop on the specimen side to secure the appendicular base.
The surgeon cuts the appendix after deciding not to use a third endo loop, due to minimal inflammation observed in the appendix.
The surgeon cauterizes the mucosa of the appendix stump and then extracts the appendix specimen through the umbilical port.
The surgeon completes a thorough visual inspection of the abdomen and terminal ileum, ensuring no residual pathology or complications are present.
The surgeon repositions the omentum near the base of the appendix, restoring it to its original anatomical location.
